name: High-Contrast Accessible
description: An accessibility-led style that treats WCAG conformance as a design driver rather than an afterthought: strong foreground/background contrast, clear non-colour state indicators, large targets, visible focus rings, and predictable, conventional patterns.
emotional_impression: Clear, dependable, inclusive, and confidently legible.
visual_principles: State communicated by icon, text, and shape, never colour alone
Large touch and click targets and generous spacing
accessibility_risks: Very high contrast can cause glare or halation for some photosensitive users, so an adjustable theme helps
Maximising contrast everywhere can flatten hierarchy if not paired with type scale and spacing
appropriate_contexts: Public-sector and regulated products with legal accessibility obligations
Any product with a broad, ageing, or low-vision user base
High-stakes transactional flows where errors are costly
inappropriate_contexts: Brand-led marketing wanting subtle, atmospheric, low-contrast mood (though it can be adapted)
Contexts that mistake 'accessible' for 'visually boring' and skip craft
evidence: WCAG 2.2 (1.4.3, 1.4.11, 2.4.7, 2.5.8) and the GOV.UK Design System exemplify this approach
GOV.UK research shows conventional, high-contrast, large-target patterns reduce error and support assistive tech
